claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 3 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 3 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I need to ask you a few questions.

Let's start with: **Do you have any children, and if so, how many children are you asking about?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I have two children - Alex who is 17, and Blake who is 3. I'd like to know if I can claim Child Benefit for them.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I can see you have two children - Alex (17) and Blake (3).

To assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for both Alex and Blake, I need to ask some questions about your circumstances.

First, let me ask about Alex since they're 17: **Is Alex currently in approved education or training?** (For example, studying at school, college, or in approved training programs)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't in any education or training at the moment. They finished school but haven't started anything else yet.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that. 

**Has Alex registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services since leaving education?** (If so, you may still be able to claim Child Benefit for up to 20 weeks)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex hasn't registered with any careers service or the armed forces.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information.

Now let me ask about your general circumstances: **Do you currently live in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK - I'm in Manchester.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, thank you. Now I need to understand the living arrangements for both children.

**Do both Alex and Blake currently live with you in Manchester?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, they both live at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Let me ask one more important question to complete my assessment:

**Has Alex (your 17-year-old) started any paid work for 24 hours or more per week, or are they receiving any benefits in their own right (such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit)?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't working that much - just does the odd bit here and there. And no, they're not receiving any benefits in their own name.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on the information you've provided, here's my assessment of your Child Benefit eligibility:

**For Blake (age 3): ✓ You ARE eligible**
Blake is under 16 and lives with you in the UK. You meet all the requirements for Child Benefit for Blake.

**For Alex (age 17): ✗ You are NOT eligible**
Unfortunately, Alex doesn't qualify because they are over 16 and not currently in approved education or training. Child Benefit for children aged 16-19 only continues if they stay in approved education or training. Since Alex has finished school and isn't in education, training, or registered with a careers service or the armed forces, you cannot claim Child Benefit for them.

**What you can do:**
- You can claim Child Benefit for Blake
- If Alex decides to return to approved education or training in the future, you may be able to claim Child Benefit for them again at that time

If you have any questions about this decision or need help with making a claim for Blake, please let me know!

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old and not currently in approved education or training. Under the Child Benefit rules, you normally qualify for a child under 16, or if the child is 16-19 and stays in approved education or training. Alex has left education and is not in training. Alex has not registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services (which would allow Child Benefit to continue for up to 20 weeks). Alex is not working 24+ hours per week and is not receiving benefits in their own right, but this does not make them eligible - the key issue is that they are over 16 and not in approved education or training. Therefore, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 3 years old, which is under 16. Blake lives with you in the UK (Manchester). You are responsible for Blake as they live with you. Blake meets all the basic eligibility criteria for Child Benefit. Therefore, you are eligible for Child Benefit for Blake.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK with both children. For Blake (age 3), all eligibility criteria are met as they are under 16 and live with the claimant. For Alex (age 17), they do not meet the eligibility criteria because they are over 16 and not in approved education or training, nor have they registered with careers services or armed forces within the 20-week grace period. The claimant is therefore eligible for Child Benefit for Blake but not for Alex.

Alex has not registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services (which would allow Child Benefit to continue for up to 20 weeks). Alex is not working 24+ hours per week and is not receiving benefits in their own right, but this does not make them eligible - the key issue is that they are over 16 and not in approved education or training. Therefore, you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

Blake is 3 years old, which is under 16. Blake lives with you in the UK (Manchester). You are responsible for Blake as they live with you. Blake meets all the basic eligibility criteria for Child Benefit. Therefore, you are eligible for Child Benefit for Blake.

For Alex (age 17), they do not meet the eligibility criteria because they are over 16 and not in approved education or training, nor have they registered with careers services or armed forces within the 20-week grace period. The claimant is therefore eligible for Child Benefit for Blake but not for Alex.
